title: The Trouble With Trillions

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening credits sequence implies Homer has had some mishap
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation and scream
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase that's perfectly integrated with his character - could only be delivered by him

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Will this horrible year never end? We've never lost a year before, and I'll be damned if we're gonna lose one on my shift.
jokes.setup: New Year's countdown is stuck on 'eight'
jokes.punchline: Character treats the year like a physical object they can lose, with professional responsibility
jokes.explanation: Clever absurd take on New Year's technical difficulties with good workplace responsibility humor

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: We're stuck on eight? Eight?! I've lost years before, but never like this!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny Leonard
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't lose a year. It's not a set of keys.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, somebody better find it before my boss asks where 2024 went. I'm gonna lose my job!

jokes.text: January 1st. Better get going on those taxes, Neddie.
jokes.setup: It's literally January 1st, the first possible day of the tax year
jokes.punchline: Ned is already thinking about taxes because he's obsessively responsible
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Ned's neurotic responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, it's literally January 1st, the first possible day of the tax year. I've already organized all my receipts by category and subcategory. Got my W-2s color-coded, my deductions alphabetized, and I've pre-filled out my 1040 form in triplicate. Feels good to be ahead of the game, yes-siree-bob!

jokes.text: Cash register ink. Well, that's a business expense, isn't it? Oh, but, then, I do enjoy the smell of the stuff, don't I? Better not risk it.
jokes.setup: Ned is doing his taxes and considering a business deduction
jokes.punchline: His moral scrupulousness extends to not claiming deductions if he personally enjoys the item
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific humor about Ned's excessive honesty, could only be him

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Well, I could deduct this office supplies expense, but I do enjoy a nice mechanical pencil. So I suppose I'll just have to pay the full amount in taxes.

jokes.text: Policemen, trees, sunshine- and let's not forget the folks who just don't feel like working, God bless 'em.
jokes.setup: Ned explaining to his children what taxes pay for
jokes.punchline: Includes welfare recipients but blesses them in his characteristically kind way
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Ned's optimistic view even of controversial topics

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Now children, taxes pay for many important services. Roads, schools, firefighters, and yes, even welfare for those less fortunate than ourselves.

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: And while some folks might take advantage of the system, we must remember to bless them and pray for their salvation, diddly-diddly-do.

jokes.text: 8:45? Here I am, yapping away like it's 8:35!
jokes.setup: Ned realizes he's running late for the post office
jokes.punchline: Ten minutes late is catastrophically irresponsible by Ned's standards
jokes.explanation: Nice character beat showing Ned's obsessive punctuality

dialogue.speaker: Ned Flanders
dialogue.line: Ten minutes late is catastrophically irresponsible by Ned's standards

jokes.text: Getting your taxes out of the way? No. Just mailing out death certificates... for holiday-related fatalities.
jokes.setup: Assumption that everyone at post office is doing last-minute taxes
jokes.punchline: Dr. Hibbert casually mentions mailing death certificates from holiday deaths
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered matter-of-factly, good contrast to the tax deadline stress

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: Well, I'm just here mailing out death certificates from all the holiday deaths. Nothing says 'festive season' like a little mortality!

jokes.text: Until you're willing to file jointly, back of the line, Seymour!
jokes.setup: Skinner compliments Edna on her appearance
jokes.punchline: Edna uses tax filing status as relationship leverage
jokes.explanation: Excellent integration of tax theme with character relationship dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: You look lovely today, Edna.

dialogue.speaker: Edna Krabappel
dialogue.line: Well, that's sweet of you to say, Seymour. But I'm afraid I can't return the sentiment unless you're willing to change your tax filing status to married.

jokes.text: Yar! Sometimes I wonder why I bother plundering at all.
jokes.setup: Sea Captain is at the post office doing taxes like everyone else
jokes.punchline: Even a pirate has to deal with modern tax bureaucracy
jokes.explanation: Great character moment contrasting pirate lifestyle with mundane tax requirements

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Even a pirate has to deal with modern tax bureaucracy

jokes.text: Oh, no. No, no. I felt that. You didn't carry the one, you foolish person. Now you'll incur the penalties with the compound interest... and the wrath and the truncheons.
jokes.setup: Professor Frink notices a mathematical error in someone's taxes
jokes.punchline: Escalates from mathematical precision to apocalyptic consequences
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Frink's mathematical obsessiveness and dramatic vocabulary

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: Good heavens! According to my calculations, you've made a decimal point error of 0.003 percent on line 47-B! Gosh!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: This microscopic mistake will cause a cascading quantum collapse in the global financial system!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: By my projections, within 72 hours, the stock market will implode, governments will fall, and the very fabric of civilization will unravel into chaos and darkness!

dialogue.speaker: Professor Frink
dialogue.line: All because of a tiny little decimal point. Egads!

jokes.text: Taxes? Isn't this the line for Metallica?
jokes.setup: Reporter asks why person waited until last minute for taxes
jokes.punchline: Person is completely confused about what line they're in
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection joke that's functional but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Reporter
dialogue.line: Why did you wait until the last minute to file your taxes?

dialogue.speaker: Random citizen
dialogue.line: Taxes? I thought this was the line for the new iPhone.

jokes.text: Because I'm an idiot! Happy?
jokes.setup: Reporter asks why person waited until last minute
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest self-assessment delivered with hostility
jokes.explanation: Relatable self-deprecating humor that cuts through the setup efficiently

dialogue.speaker: Random Citizen
dialogue.line: I'm an idiot.

jokes.text: And at the risk of sounding a little smug- Ohh! Help! Does anyone have a calculator?
jokes.setup: Kent boasts about being prepared with an accountant
jokes.punchline: Immediately needs help with basic calculations
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff with immediate karmic punishment for smugness

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: I'm always prepared. In fact, I have an accountant on speed dial.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Say, what's two plus two?

dialogue.speaker: Accountant
dialogue.line: Four.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: See? Prepared!

jokes.text: I paid my taxes over a year ago.
jokes.setup: Homer smugly criticizes the 'morons' rushing to file taxes
jokes.punchline: Sets up that Homer thinks last year's taxes count for this year
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Homer's fundamental misunderstanding of how taxes work

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at all those morons rushing to file their taxes.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you haven't filed yours yet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's because I'm not a moron. I already paid taxes last year.

jokes.text: What is it, sweetie? Did you see a scary picture in your picture book?
jokes.setup: Lisa is trying to tell Homer something important
jokes.punchline: Homer patronizingly assumes she's scared by a children's book
jokes.explanation: Shows Homer's obliviousness to Lisa's intelligence level

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, I need to tell you something important.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What is it, sweetie? Did that scary book give you nightmares again?

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No, Dad, it's not about—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, there's no such thing as the Big Bad Wolf. Well, except in the woods. And the zoo. And that one time at the Kwik-E-Mart.

jokes.text: That was last year's taxes. You have to pay again this year.
jokes.setup: Homer's previous smugness about having paid taxes
jokes.punchline: Lisa explains the basic concept that taxes are annual
jokes.explanation: Payoff to Homer's misunderstanding, shows Lisa's role as the smart one

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, taxes are annual. You have to pay them every year.

jokes.text: No. Because, you see, I went ahead, and... year-wise, I was counting forward from the last previous- D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer tries to rationalize his tax confusion
jokes.punchline: His attempt at explanation dissolves into nonsense, ending with his signature exclamation
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer moment of attempted intelligence followed by realization of stupidity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, taxes are very complicated. There's the federal tax, the state tax, the local tax... it's all very confusing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So I just picked a number, sent it in, and hoped for the best. D'oh!

jokes.text: I have a 'to do' pile?
jokes.setup: Marge mentions she put tax forms on his to-do pile a month ago
jokes.punchline: Homer is genuinely surprised to learn he has organizational systems
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment showing his complete disconnect from household organization

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A to-do pile? I have a to-do pile?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, Homer. The pile on the left side of your desk.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not a to-do pile, Marge. That's a 'to-don't' pile.

jokes.text: Marge, how many kids do we have? Oh, no time to count. I'll just estimate- uh, nine.
jokes.setup: Homer is rushing to do his taxes
jokes.punchline: Doesn't know how many children he has and makes a wildly inaccurate estimate
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurd escalation - even basic family facts are unclear to Homer

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... how many kids do I have? One, two, three... I'm gonna say four!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we have three children.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, the government doesn't know that!

jokes.text: If I don't hear you, it's not illegal.
jokes.setup: Marge starts to correct Homer's tax mistakes
jokes.punchline: Homer's completely nonsensical legal theory
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic that's completely absurd but delivered with complete confidence

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you can't just write 'oops' on your tax return.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why not? It's honest! And I've got a legal theory that says the IRS has to accept it because the word 'taxes' has the word 'ax' in it, and axes are tools, and tools are made in factories, and factories are run by the government, so technically the government owes me money!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That makes no sense whatsoever.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! That's why they can't argue with it!

jokes.text: Business gifts! Here you go. Keep using nuclear power.
jokes.setup: Homer looking for tax deductions
jokes.punchline: Claims Lisa's painting as a business gift with inappropriate message
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's work bleeding into family life inappropriately

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... I can deduct this as a business gift.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa painted this beautiful landscape, and I'm giving it to my boss Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Now, what message should I write on the back?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How about... 'To Mr. Burns, may your heart be as dark as this painting is ugly'?

jokes.text: you require 24-hour nursing care, Lisa's a clergyman... Maggie is seven people, and Bart was wounded in Vietnam.
jokes.setup: Homer telling Marge what fake deductions to claim
jokes.punchline: Each family member gets an increasingly absurd fake status
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating absurdity with perfect pacing - each claim more ridiculous than the last

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay Marge, here's what we'll do. You claim Bart as a dependent farm animal.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's ridiculous!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa can be a home office. And Maggie—we'll write her off as a charitable donation.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We can't do that!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll list myself as a small business—Homers R Us. And you, Marge, you're a moving expense.

jokes.text: Cool!
jokes.setup: Homer claims Bart was wounded in Vietnam for tax purposes
jokes.punchline: Bart thinks being a Vietnam veteran sounds cool
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart reaction - finds the dangerous/rebellious aspect appealing

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Son, I'm claiming you as a wounded Vietnam veteran on our taxes.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, cool! I'm a Vietnam vet? That's awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, not really. I just need the deduction.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Aw man, you got my hopes up.

jokes.text: You really had a lot of talent, kid.
jokes.setup: Homer has just claimed Lisa's painting as a business gift
jokes.punchline: Speaks of her talent in past tense as if her art career is over
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan moment treating the tax fraud as ending Lisa's art career

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa had such a gift for painting. It's a shame she gave it up.

jokes.text: If I don't see it, it's not illegal!
jokes.setup: Callback to earlier nonsensical legal theory
jokes.punchline: Homer yells it while driving recklessly to the post office
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates the absurd legal theory to dangerous driving

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm on my way to the post office and I'm obeying all traffic laws... in my mind!

jokes.text: So, Lenny, let's say you pull a thorn out of the pope's butt, and he grants you one wish. What'll it be?
jokes.setup: Homer poses a hypothetical scenario to friends
jokes.punchline: Combines religious irreverence with magical wish-granting premise
jokes.explanation: Very creative absurd premise that's memorable and quotable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So if I had three wishes, I'd wish for a million bucks, a genie girlfriend, and for all the people I hate to die in their sleep.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: You're sick, man.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm not killing anybody. I'm just wishing it.

jokes.text: Well, I've always wondered what it would feel like to wear something that's been ironed.
jokes.setup: Lenny gets one wish from the pope
jokes.punchline: His ultimate desire is basic clothing care he's never experienced
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment revealing Lenny's simple working-class life through one detail

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: I wish for someone to do my laundry!

jokes.text: That'd be sweet. What about you, Moe? Ah, gee, I was gonna say a night with Joey Heatherton. But an ironed shirt? Damn, that's tempting!
jokes.setup: Moe had a predictable male fantasy wish ready
jokes.punchline: Lenny's mundane wish makes Moe reconsider his priorities
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how Lenny's wish puts everyone's priorities in perspective

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: So I get three wishes, right? First thing I'm wishin' for is a night with three beautiful women.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: I wish I had a sandwich.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: A sandwich? That's it? You know what, forget my wishes. I want a sandwich too.

jokes.text: Homer Simpson? United States government. Help! Somebody! Help!
jokes.setup: Government agent approaches Homer at the bar
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately panics and calls for help
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's immediate guilt and panic

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: Homer Simpson?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh God, what did I do? Barkeep! Barkeep! Call the police!

jokes.text: This is an egregious miscarriagement of taxitude.
jokes.setup: Homer being questioned about tax fraud
jokes.punchline: Invents fake legal/tax terminology to sound smart
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - trying to sound intelligent with made-up words

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm invoking the principle of chronological tax avoidance and filing under subsection... uh... purple.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Plus, I have a receipt from the Department of Monetary Forgiveness. See? It's right here in crayon.

jokes.text: Mr. Simpson, this government computer can process over nine tax returns per day.
jokes.setup: Agent explaining government efficiency to Homer
jokes.punchline: Nine returns per day is presented as impressive computing power
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan satire of government inefficiency presented as achievement

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: We can process nine returns per day with this system.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nine?! That's... that's amazing!

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: Well, it's government efficiency.

jokes.text: No, sir. I'm really sorry, sir. An older boy told me to do it.
jokes.setup: Homer facing serious tax fraud charges
jokes.punchline: Reverts to childhood excuse-making
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer reaction - adult man using playground logic in federal investigation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I didn't do it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nobody saw me do it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You can't prove anything!

jokes.text: No, sir. Please, I can't go to prison. They pee in a cup and throw it on you! I saw it in a movie.
jokes.setup: Homer pleading to avoid prison
jokes.punchline: His prison knowledge comes from movies and focuses on bizarre hazing
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment showing his skewed understanding of reality from pop culture

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Please, Your Honor, I don't want to go to prison! I've seen what happens there. The hazing, the terrible things... They make you fight the biggest guy, they steal your lunch money, and worst of all, they make you shower with everybody watching!

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: That's... not quite how it works, Mr. Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And don't even get me started on what they do with the soap in the shower!

jokes.text: You won't be seeing any prison movies where you're going- prison!
jokes.setup: Seems like agent might be offering alternative to prison
jokes.punchline: Misdirection reveals he's just emphasizing that prison means prison
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection joke with good structure

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: You have two choices: prison, or you can work for us.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, work for you? That sounds great!

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: No, no. Prison means prison.

jokes.text: Okay, but could you pay me under the table? I got a little tax problem.
jokes.setup: Homer agrees to work for the government
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks for illegal payment while being prosecuted for tax crimes
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic moment showing Homer's complete failure to learn from his situation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So I work for the government now?

dialogue.speaker: Government Official
dialogue.line: That's correct, Mr. Simpson. You'll be starting immediately.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Great! So what's my under-the-table cash payment gonna be?

dialogue.speaker: Government Official
dialogue.line: Under-the-table? This is a government job!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But I've still got those back taxes...

jokes.text: They want you to spy on your friends? Not spy, squeal. I've got no choice.
jokes.setup: Marge asks if Homer has to spy
jokes.punchline: Homer corrects her with a more demeaning but accurate term
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's blunt honesty about his degrading situation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Do you have to spy on people?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not a spy, Marge. I'm a peeping tom.

jokes.text: Quiet, honey. You don't know how big this government is. It goes all the way to the president!
jokes.setup: Marge objects to government using Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer reveals the 'shocking' fact that government includes the president
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment treating basic civics knowledge as conspiracy theory revelation

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I can't believe the government is using you!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Using me? Marge, the government is a lot of things, but it's not like they're just using one guy. It includes the president!

jokes.text: There you go, ratboy! Does this make me look fat? No. It makes you look like a tool of government oppression. But not fat?
jokes.setup: Homer tries on a wire, Marge calls him a tool of oppression
jokes.punchline: Homer only cares about the appearance issue, not the moral one
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's vanity overriding moral concerns

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you're a tool of oppression!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A tool? Where?

jokes.text: Hey, I see you're watching a ball game. Looks like a good one. Any of you involved in any illegal activity? 'Cause I could sure go for some.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to secretly record his friends
jokes.punchline: His approach is completely transparent and incriminating to himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer incompetence - world's worst undercover work combined with self-incrimination

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Guys, I'm just gonna put this walkie-talkie in my shirt pocket... completely inconspicuously.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, we can see the antenna sticking out of your mouth.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No you can't.

jokes.text: How about you, Lenny? Testing, testing. Lenny?
jokes.setup: Homer wearing a wire trying to get his friends to confess crimes
jokes.punchline: Obviously testing his recording equipment
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - Homer being terrible at undercover work

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is this thing on? Hello? Hello? Can you hear me? I'm wearing a wire.

jokes.text: You mean like the time you was running moonshine out of your basement? Or that telemarketing scam you pulled? Uh, like those. But involving you.
jokes.setup: Homer asks for examples of illegal activities
jokes.punchline: Lenny lists Homer's own crimes, Homer redirects wanting to hear about others' crimes
jokes.explanation: Good ironic moment revealing Homer's extensive criminal history while he's trying to entrap others

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Give me some examples of illegal activities.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Well, there's armed robbery, grand theft auto, operating a nuclear power plant without a license...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I mean other people's crimes!

jokes.text: You mean like the time Barney beat up George Bush? Barney? That was me! And I'd do it again.
jokes.setup: Lenny tries to give Homer a crime example involving Barney
jokes.punchline: Homer corrects that he committed the assault and threatens to repeat it
jokes.explanation: Good callback with escalation - Homer can't resist taking credit for his crimes

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, like that time Barney committed that assault?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That was me! And if you don't shut up, I'll do it again!

jokes.text: Why stop there, Homer? My militia has a secret plan... to beat up all sorts of government officials. That'll teach 'em to drag their feet on high definition TV!
jokes.setup: Moe reveals he has a militia with plans to attack government
jokes.punchline: The motivation is impatience about consumer technology
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with absurd motivation - violent extremism over TV technology

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Yeah, we got this whole militia thing planned out. We're gonna strike at the government, show 'em what's what.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Moe, that's pretty serious. What's the motivation?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: They keep delaying the release of the new iPhone!

jokes.text: Oh, that's ridiculous, Moe. End transmission.
jokes.setup: Homer realizes he's been exposed as wearing a wire
jokes.punchline: Tries to end the recording as if it's a phone call or radio transmission
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment misunderstanding how surveillance technology works

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that's all for now. Over and out.

jokes.text: You have a flair for treachery.
jokes.setup: Agent praising Homer's undercover work
jokes.punchline: Presents betrayal of friends as a professional skill
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan moment treating moral failings as career assets

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: Simpson, your undercover work has been exemplary. You've successfully infiltrated the criminal underworld and earned their trust.

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: That kind of betrayal of your friends and loved ones—that's a rare talent. We'd like to offer you a permanent position in the bureau.

jokes.text: Why do you keep picking on me? Marge's name was on that tax return too.
jokes.setup: Homer being given another assignment
jokes.punchline: Tries to deflect by throwing wife under the bus
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's willingness to sacrifice family to save himself

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Why are you giving this to me? Marge is the organized one. She's always doing stuff. I just sit around.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, ask Marge. She loves this kind of thing.

jokes.text: Uh, m-my shirt fell off.
jokes.setup: Homer being photographed entering FBI building
jokes.punchline: Tries to hide his identity with implausible excuse for covering his face
jokes.explanation: Simple character moment showing Homer's bad attempt at maintaining cover

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not hiding from the FBI! I'm just allergic to buildings!

jokes.text: Losers.
jokes.setup: Educational film about Europeans struggling to rebuild after WWII
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissively calls war victims losers
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer insensitivity delivered with perfect economy - historically ignorant and callous

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Losers!

jokes.text: who fought so poorly and surrendered so readily.
jokes.setup: Educational film about helping European allies
jokes.punchline: Narrator insults the allies America is supposedly helping
jokes.explanation: Good satire of American attitudes disguised as official educational material

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And so, America sends aid to help our European allies overcome the crushing weight of poverty and war.

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: Of course, these pathetic, ungrateful nations will never appreciate the sacrifices we make for them.

jokes.text: To make good on this drunken boast...
jokes.setup: Narrator describing Truman's promise to help Europe
jokes.punchline: Describes major foreign policy initiative as drunk promise
jokes.explanation: Good satirical reframing of historical foreign policy as impulsive decision-making

dialogue.speaker: Narrator
dialogue.line: And so, President Truman made a solemn promise to help rebuild Europe after the war. A promise he would keep... even though he made it while absolutely three sheets to the wind at a diplomatic cocktail party.

jokes.text: Ooh, a trillion-dollar bill. That's a spicy meatball!
jokes.setup: Film shows the trillion-dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Homer responds with completely unrelated Italian stereotype phrase
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer non-sequitur mixing inappropriate cultural reference with serious moment

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mamma mia!

jokes.text: Ooh, a trillion-dollar bill. That's a spicy meatball!
jokes.setup: Seeing the trillion-dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Random Italian stereotype exclamation
jokes.explanation: Typical Homer non-sequitur but relies on cheap ethnic stereotype

jokes.text: Well, this is a kick in the knickers. Should we complain to somebody? No. I say we just act snooty to Americans forever! I agree.
jokes.setup: Europeans learn the money never arrived
jokes.punchline: Their response is to be perpetually snooty rather than seek justice
jokes.explanation: Good satirical explanation for European attitude toward Americans

dialogue.speaker: European Official 1
dialogue.line: The money never arrived?

dialogue.speaker: European Official 2
dialogue.line: How absolutely dreadful. Well, I suppose there's nothing to be done about it now.

dialogue.speaker: European Official 1
dialogue.line: Quite right. We shall simply have to maintain our air of superiority and continue looking down our noses at Americans.

dialogue.speaker: European Official 2
dialogue.line: A most satisfactory resolution. One doesn't need justice when one has smugness.

jokes.text: But all we've ascertained from satellite photos is that it's not on the roof.
jokes.setup: Agent explaining their investigation of Burns
jokes.punchline: Massive surveillance technology used to determine obvious fact
jokes.explanation: Excellent satire of government inefficiency and over-reliance on technology

dialogue.speaker: Agent Johnson
dialogue.line: We used massive surveillance technology, satellite imaging, and wiretaps to determine that Mr. Burns is... evil.

jokes.text: But Mr. Burns gave me my job, and he hasn't fired me... even after three meltdowns and one China Syndrome. I can't betray him.
jokes.setup: Homer showing loyalty to Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's caused multiple nuclear disasters but wasn't fired
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - loyalty based on gratitude for not being fired despite catastrophic incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I'd do anything for you! I'm completely loyal!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Is that so, Simpson? Even after you've caused multiple nuclear disasters at the plant?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, yeah! You haven't fired me yet, have you?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Actually, I have. Fourteen times. You kept showing up the next day.

jokes.text: Oh, stop fishing for compliments, Smithers. Go home to your can of mushroom soup.
jokes.setup: Smithers serves elaborate meal hoping for praise
jokes.punchline: Burns dismisses him cruelly while referencing his sad home life
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns cruelty - dismissive of effort and revealing knowledge of Smithers' lonely life

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, this roasted duck is as dry as the Sahara. And these potatoes are lumpy — much like your mother's complexion, I'm told.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, I... I spent all night preparing this meal.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, you wasted your time. Now get out of my sight. Go home to that empty apartment of yours and contemplate your failures.

jokes.text: I'm in.
jokes.setup: Homer attempting stealth entry into Burns' mansion
jokes.punchline: Announces his arrival like a professional spy despite being obvious
jokes.explanation: Simple spy movie parody showing Homer's incompetence at stealth

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm in. Code name: Spy Guy. The eagle has landed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, why am I announcing this out loud?

jokes.text: A pack of vicious dogs should be ripping you to pieces.
jokes.setup: Burns surprised Homer got past his security
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions he expected dogs to attack his employee
jokes.explanation: Good Burns moment showing his casual attitude toward employee safety

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm surprised you made it past my security, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I had the dogs set to 'attack' this morning.

jokes.text: Well, Mr. Burns, you always come off as kind of a gruff, crotchety loner. But we both know that deep down inside- Ha-ha!
jokes.setup: Homer tries to get Burns to open up emotionally
jokes.punchline: Burns laughs at the suggestion he has any warmth inside
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Burns' complete lack of hidden warmth

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Come on, Mr. Burns. Don't you have any warmth inside you?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Warmth? Ha! The only thing warm inside me is my hatred for the working class.

jokes.text: Come on! Where are you? Here, trilli-trilli!
jokes.setup: Homer searching for the trillion-dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Calls to it like a pet using baby talk
jokes.explanation: Good Homer absurdity treating the bill like a lost pet

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Here, trillion-dollar bill! Here, boy! Come on, sweetie! Mommy's got a shiny new wallet for you!

jokes.text: Oh, I get it. I'm onto you. You're from Collier's magazine, aren't you? Are you going to put me in 'Star Snoop'?
jokes.setup: Burns catches Homer snooping around his house
jokes.punchline: Assumes he's from a long-defunct magazine and outdated gossip column
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment showing his complete disconnect from modern times

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Homer! What are you doing in my house?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was just... looking around, Mr. Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Looking around? You must be from that old Confidential magazine! Or perhaps you're writing for one of those outdated gossip columns. Well, I have news for you — those publications folded decades ago, just like your credibility!

jokes.text: Well, of course, I didn't want a racehorse in my living room. But you don't say no to Spring Byington, do you?
jokes.setup: Burns showing Homer around his mansion
jokes.punchline: References obscure 1940s actress as if she's currently relevant
jokes.explanation: Good Burns anachronism - references forgotten actress as current celebrity

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And here is my prized collection of paintings by obscure 1940s actresses. Ah, Veronica Lake - still a vision of loveliness, wouldn't you say?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, sir, I think she's been dead for like thirty years.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense! She's probably just resting. Now, if you'll excuse me, I have a dinner reservation with Joan Crawford at eight.

jokes.text: Oh, you'll find this amusing- the suit Charlie Chaplin was buried in.
jokes.setup: Burns showing off his collection
jokes.punchline: Casually owns dead celebrity's burial clothes
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor showing Burns' morbid collecting habits

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And here we have the burial outfit of Elvis Presley. I acquired it for a steal—literally stole it from Graceland.

jokes.text: tossing that tea without a care... for what the caffeine would do to the Fenway flounder.
jokes.setup: Burns explaining his ancestor's role in Boston Tea Party
jokes.punchline: Bizarre concern for effect of tea on non-existent fish
jokes.explanation: Creative absurd historical revision with made-up environmental concern

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: My ancestor, Cornelius Burns, was a key participant in the Boston Tea Party. He didn't care about taxation without representation—he just wanted to prevent the tea from poisoning the fish.

jokes.text: It commemorates my liberation of a trillion dollars that would have been foolishly squandered.
jokes.setup: Burns explaining a portrait showing him with the trillion-dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Reframes theft as patriotic 'liberation' of money
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment - reframing crime as public service with complete conviction

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: This portrait commemorates the day I liberated a trillion dollars from the Federal Reserve.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Some call it theft. I call it patriotic wealth redistribution... to myself.

jokes.text: Every year you make hardworking joes like my reporter friend here pay income taxes. And for what? Aid to ungrateful foreigners... do-nothing nuclear missiles... tomb polish for some unknown soldier.
jokes.setup: Burns defending himself to FBI agents
jokes.punchline: Lists absurd government expenditures including tomb maintenance
jokes.explanation: Good political satire with escalating absurdity, ending with silly tomb maintenance

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You call those expenditures wasteful? Why, I've seen the government throw away millions on tomb maintenance alone!

jokes.text: Hey, he's right. You crooks in Washington-
jokes.setup: Homer was working for the government
jokes.punchline: Immediately switches sides when Burns makes his speech
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's complete lack of loyalty or consistency

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Homer, I have an offer for you. Work for me, and I'll pay you triple what the government pays.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, triple? I'm in! Where do I sign?

jokes.text: Oh, you can silence me, but you can't silence Collier's magazine. Tell the people! Don't let the government push you around! You have a choice. Fight back! I'm gonna write the best darn article- Oh, wait.
jokes.setup: Homer gives passionate speech about fighting government oppression
jokes.punchline: Remembers he's not actually a reporter and deflates
jokes.explanation: Good escalation/deflation structure with callback to earlier mistaken identity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We must fight against government oppression! Stand up for your rights! Question authority!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait... I'm not actually a reporter.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Never mind.

jokes.text: We'll hide out at my place. I've got beer.
jokes.setup: Homer and Burns need to hide from FBI
jokes.punchline: Homer's primary selling point for his hideout is alcohol
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's priorities even during crisis

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I know the perfect place to hide. It's got everything we need.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And what would that be, Simpson?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Beer.

jokes.text: Uh, that really was more of a burgundy.
jokes.setup: Police radio describes their car as maroon
jokes.punchline: Smithers corrects the color designation while being hunted
jokes.explanation: Perfect Smithers moment - obsessive attention to detail even during escape

dialogue.speaker: Police Radio
dialogue.line: We're looking for a maroon sedan.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Actually, it's burgundy.

jokes.text: We'll purchase a small island somewhere and start our own country... free from the relentless tyranny of Uncle Sam.
jokes.setup: Burns and Homer discussing escape plans
jokes.punchline: Burns' solution is to literally leave America and start new country
jokes.explanation: Good Burns moment showing his grandiose solutions to problems

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, we must escape this country at once. I propose we flee to a remote island and establish our own sovereign nation.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Mr. Burns, what about our families? What about our homes?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Bah! Sentimental drivel. I've already purchased three Caribbean islands and hired a team of mercenaries to enforce my rule. We'll call it 'Burnsia.' I'll be Supreme Leader, naturally. You can be Minister of Donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Minister of Donuts? When do I start?

jokes.text: What about my wife and kids? That can be shipped.
jokes.setup: Homer worried about leaving his family
jokes.punchline: Burns treats family like cargo that can be mailed
jokes.explanation: Good Burns callousness treating humans like objects with perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I'm worried about leaving my family.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Don't be ridiculous, Simpson. Family is just cargo that can be mailed.

jokes.text: My dad has a trillion dollars? Wow! I can buy and sell your sorry ass.
jokes.setup: Marge asks kids where Homer would go with trillion dollars
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately becomes arrogant with imagined wealth
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing how money immediately corrupts Bart

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Where do you think your father would go if he had a trillion dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Spend it all on himself, man. He'd probably buy like a billion donuts or something.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: He'd waste it on beer and lottery tickets.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah, and then he'd come crawling back to me asking for money. Not happening, dude. When I'm rich, I'm not sharing with nobody.

jokes.text: Who needs college, Mom? We're trillionaires! Let's buy dune buggies!
jokes.setup: Marge mentions saving money for college
jokes.punchline: Bart rejects education in favor of recreational vehicles
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment showing his preference for fun over responsibility

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We need to start saving money for college.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: College? Ay caramba! I'd rather have a sweet RV, man.

jokes.text: Turn back immediately or we will be unable to prosecute you.
jokes.setup: FBI warning escaping criminals at border
jokes.punchline: Threatens them with inability to pursue justice
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd reversal - law enforcement warning criminals they might escape justice

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Stop right there! You're under arrest for crossing the border illegally.

dialogue.speaker: Criminal
dialogue.line: You can't touch us now—we're in Mexico!

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: That's right. And if you think that's bad, wait until you see what happens when you try to come back—I won't be able to pursue justice!

jokes.text: We better do what he says! Wait! We're now over international waters. Whoo-hoo! We can gamble! Yes!
jokes.setup: Homer initially wants to obey FBI warning
jokes.punchline: Changes mind when he realizes he can gamble legally
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - fear of law overcome by prospect of minor vice

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, the FBI warning says I can't copy this tape... but it doesn't say I can't gamble!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Legal gambling!

jokes.text: They'll be back. They'll miss American TV.
jokes.setup: FBI agent watching criminals escape to freedom
jokes.punchline: Predicts return due to entertainment quality, not freedom
jokes.explanation: Good cultural commentary suggesting TV is more addictive than freedom is valuable

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: They'll be back.

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: Not because of freedom — because the ratings on their escape were too good.

jokes.text: And it has 'freedom' written all over it. Sir, that's Cuba.
jokes.setup: Homer excited about finding island with 'freedom'
jokes.punchline: Smithers reveals it's the communist country
jokes.explanation: Good political irony - seeking freedom in country known for lacking it

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: An island! With freedom! Finally, a place where I can be myself!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Actually, sir, that's Cuba.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Cuba? But you said freedom!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Yes, well, they call it that.

jokes.text: Sorry about the landing, boys. This fog is so thick, I can't see my own cataracts.
jokes.setup: Burns apologizing for bad plane landing
jokes.punchline: Blames fog but reveals his vision problems are medical
jokes.explanation: Good Burns moment combining excuse-making with admission of extreme old age

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I apologize for that rather bumpy landing. The fog was simply ghastly.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Though, to be honest, I can't see more than three feet in front of my face on a clear day.

jokes.text: It's carne de burro. Nice to meet you.
jokes.setup: Homer mentions shredded pork everywhere
jokes.punchline: Cuban corrects that it's donkey meat
jokes.explanation: Simple cultural humor about food quality in Cuba

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Shredded pork everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Cuban official
dialogue.line: Actually, it's donkey meat.

jokes.text: Comrades, our nation is completely bankrupt. We have no choice but to abandon communism. I know, I know, I know. But we all knew from day one this mumbo jumbo wouldn't fly.
jokes.setup: Castro addressing his people about economic crisis
jokes.punchline: Casually admits communism was never expected to work
jokes.explanation: Good political satire treating communist ideology as obvious scam

dialogue.speaker: Fidel Castro
dialogue.line: My people, we face an economic crisis. But do not despair—communism was never expected to work anyway.

jokes.text: Ah, they're not so bad. They even named a street after me in San Francisco. It's full of what?
jokes.setup: Castro defending Americans despite assassination attempts
jokes.punchline: Learns San Francisco's Castro Street has different significance
jokes.explanation: Good cultural joke about Castro not understanding what Castro Street represents

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: I have defended Americans despite many assassination attempts. Your country owes me a debt.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, we're very grateful. Tell you what, we'll name a street after you.

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: Really? A street named after me?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's in San Francisco. Castro Street.

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: Ah, excellent! I will visit this street named in my honor.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Uh, maybe you should just send a postcard.

jokes.text: ¡Ay, caramba!
jokes.setup: Castro learning about the trillion-dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Uses Bart Simpson's signature exclamation
jokes.explanation: Fun crossover moment having Cuban leader use signature Simpsons catchphrase

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: A trillion-dollar bill? Ay caramba!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ay caramba!

jokes.text: See with your eyes, not with your hands.
jokes.setup: Castro asking to examine the trillion-dollar bill
jokes.punchline: Gives parental instruction about appropriate touching
jokes.explanation: Simple humor treating dictator like parent giving child instructions

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: May I examine the bill?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure.

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: This is a remarkable document. Tell me, do you know what this bill is worth?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A trillion dollars?

dialogue.speaker: Castro
dialogue.line: Exactly. And do you know what that means? It means you must be very careful about who touches it. Only appropriate people should handle such a valuable item. You must teach your children this lesson.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay.

jokes.text: It's hard to believe there's a place worse than America. But we found it.
jokes.setup: Homer reflecting on their time in Cuba
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment to America while insulting Cuba
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's complex relationship with his country

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, we may not have everything in America, but at least we don't have to worry about Castro showing up at our door demanding we fix his toilet.

jokes.text: Oppression and harassment are a small price to pay to live in the land of the free.
jokes.setup: Burns expressing renewed appreciation for America
jokes.punchline: Describes freedom as including oppression and harassment
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic patriotic sentiment that undermines itself

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about what makes America great. It's freedom. The freedom to pursue your dreams, the freedom to speak your mind, the freedom to oppress and sexually harass anyone you please.

jokes.text: Well, if it's a crime to love one's country, then I'm guilty. And if it's a crime to steal a trillion dollars from our government... and hand it over to Communist Cuba, then I'm guilty of that too.
jokes.setup: Burns defending himself with patriotic rhetoric
jokes.punchline: Equates patriotism with actual treason in the same breath
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with perfect structure - starts patriotic, escalates to treasonous admission

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm a patriot! I love this country. Why, I'd die for it... well, not die exactly, but I'd definitely be willing to commit treason against it.

jokes.text: And if it's a crime to bribe a jury... then so help me... I'll soon be guilty of that! God bless America!
jokes.setup: Burns already admitted to treason
jokes.punchline: Threatens jury tampering while invoking God's blessing on America
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation ending - threatens future crimes while expressing patriotism

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I move that we adjourn this proceeding and instead settle this matter with a duel. Pistols at dawn!

dialogue.speaker: Judge
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, you can't challenge the entire judicial system to a duel.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Oh, very well. Then I'll simply bribe each juror individually. And God bless America.

tmdb_overview: Facing an IRS audit, Homer agrees to help the feds bust Mr. Burns for a missing trillion-dollar bill. When Burns makes an anti-government stand, Homer flees with him to Cuba.
insight: Absurdist escalation carries 88 jokes across 22 minutes—just shy of elite territory.
